title: A Skew Stochastic Heat Equation
overall_summary: The paper constructs and analyzes a Markov solution to a stochastic heat equation on [0,1] with Dirichlet boundary conditions and a singular spatial local-time drift, associated with a bounded bounded-variation potential f. Its central object is the explicit Gibbs-type measure őĹ(dx)=Z^{-1} exp(-‚ąę_0^1 f(x_r)dr) őľ(dx), where őľ is Brownian bridge law, and the symmetric Dirichlet form E(ŌÜ,Ōą)=1/2‚ąę‚ü®‚ąáŌÜ,‚ąáŌą‚ü©dőĹ. The main techniques are integration by parts for Brownian bridge local times, Fukushima decomposition and Revuz measures, spectral comparison with the Ornstein-Uhlenbeck Dirichlet form, absolute-continuity estimates for the resolvent, and Mosco convergence of regularized or finite-dimensional approximations. For the original problem, the most useful content is the Gibbs invariant measure construction for singular drifts that are distributional derivatives of bounded potentials, the proof that bounded perturbations of Brownian bridge Dirichlet forms inherit Hilbert-Schmidt kernels and resolvent absolute continuity, and the approximation scheme that passes invariant stationary laws to singular limits. The paper does not prove strong Feller properties or uniqueness for general weak solutions; indeed it explicitly states that uniqueness for general f seems out of reach, so its contribution is mainly a canonical reversible construction and convergence theory rather than an ergodicity theorem.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let H=L^2(0,1), let A=1/2 d^2/dőł^2 with Dirichlet boundary conditions, and let őľ be the law on H of the standard Brownian bridge from 0 to 0 on [0,1]. The covariance of őľ is Q=(-2A)^{-1}, with eigenvectors e_k(őł)=sqrt(2) sin(kŌÄőł) and eigenvalues őĽ_k=(ŌÄk)^{-2}. Let E_0(ŌÜ,Ōą)=1/2‚ąę_H ‚ü®‚ąáŌÜ,‚ąáŌą‚ü© dőľ be the Gaussian Dirichlet form, closed from the exponential trigonometric core Exp_A(H). If őď is the set of finitely supported multi-indices ő≥:N*‚ÜíN, then there is a complete orthonormal system (H_ő≥)_{ő≥‚ąąőď} in L^2(őľ) such that E_0(ŌÜ,ŌÜ)=ő£_{ő≥‚ąąőď} őõ_ő≥ ‚ü®ŌÜ,H_ő≥‚ü©_{L^2(őľ)}^2 for all ŌÜ‚ąąD(E_0), where őõ_ő≥=ő£_{k‚Č•1} ő≥_k őĽ_k^{-1}=ő£_{k‚Č•1} ő≥_k ŌÄ^2 k^2. In particular the embedding D(E_0) into L^2(őľ) is compact.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is the standard spectral decomposition of the Ornstein-Uhlenbeck Dirichlet form associated with the Brownian bridge Gaussian measure. Diagonalize the covariance operator Q in the sine basis e_k. The functions H_ő≥ are the corresponding products of one-dimensional Hermite polynomials in the Gaussian coordinates ‚ü®x,e_k‚ü©, normalized in L^2(őľ). The Ornstein-Uhlenbeck generator is diagonal on this chaos basis, with eigenvalue őõ_ő≥ on H_ő≥. Since őõ_ő≥ tends to infinity and has finite multiplicities at each level, the unit ball of D(E_0), whose norm weights Fourier-Hermite coefficients by 1+őõ_ő≥, is relatively compact in L^2(őľ).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For the Ornstein-Uhlenbeck semigroup P_t^0 associated with E_0 in L^2(őľ), P_t^0 is Hilbert-Schmidt for every t>0. More precisely, ő£_{ő≥‚ąąőď} exp(-2őõ_ő≥ t)=ő†_{k=1}^‚ąě (1-exp(-2tŌÄ^2 k^2))^{-1}<‚ąě. Consequently p_t^0(x,y)=ő£_{ő≥‚ąąőď} exp(-őõ_ő≥ t) H_ő≥(x)H_ő≥(y) converges in L^2(őľ‚äóőľ) and gives an integral kernel P_t^0 ŌÜ(x)=‚ąę ŌÜ(y)p_t^0(x,y) őľ(dy) for őľ-a.e. x and all ŌÜ‚ąąL^2(őľ).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Group the multi-indices ő≥ according to n=ő£_k ő≥_k k^2 and let C_n be the number of such ő≥. Then ő£_ő≥ exp(-2őõ_ő≥ t)=ő£_{n‚Č•0} C_n exp(-2ŌÄ^2tn). Euler's generating-function identity gives ő£_{n‚Č•0} C_n r^n=ő†_{k‚Č•1}(1-r^{k^2})^{-1} for |r|<1. With r=exp(-2ŌÄ^2t), the infinite product converges because -log(1-r^{k^2}) is asymptotic to r^{k^2}, which is summable. A symmetric Hilbert-Schmidt operator on L^2 has an L^2 kernel, yielding the displayed representation.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The compact embedding D(E_0) into L^2(őľ) is not Hilbert-Schmidt.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: In the spectral basis H_ő≥, the embedding would be Hilbert-Schmidt exactly if ő£_{ő≥‚Č†0} őõ_ő≥^{-1}<‚ąě. With the same counting numbers C_n as in Proposition 2.2, this sum equals ő£_{n‚Č•1} C_n/n. Using the generating function Ōá(r)=ő£ C_n r^n=ő†_k(1-r^{k^2})^{-1}, one has ő£_{n‚Č•1} C_n/n=‚ąę_0^1 (Ōá(r)-1)/r dr. The integral diverges near r=1 because Ōá(r)‚Č•(1-r)^{-1}. Thus compactness is available but Hilbert-Schmidt compactness of the embedding itself is not.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let f:R‚ÜíR be bounded and of bounded variation, set F(x)=‚ąę_0^1 f(x_r)dr for x‚ąąH, and define őĹ(dx)=Z^{-1} exp(-F(x)) őľ(dx). Let K=Exp_A(H), the linear span of 1, cos(‚ü®¬∑,h‚ü©), and sin(‚ü®¬∑,h‚ü©) with h‚ąąD(A). The symmetric bilinear form E(ŌÜ,Ōą)=1/2‚ąę_H ‚ü®‚ąáŌÜ,‚ąáŌą‚ü© dőĹ, initially on K, is closable in L^2(őĹ); its closure is denoted (E,D(E)).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Because f is bounded, the density dőĹ/dőľ is bounded above and below by positive constants. Hence L^2(őĹ) and L^2(őľ) norms are equivalent, and the graph norms E_1 and E_{0,1} are equivalent up to constants on K. Since the Gaussian form (E_0,K) is closable in L^2(őľ), any E-Cauchy sequence in K that converges to zero in L^2(őĹ) is also E_0-Cauchy and converges to zero in L^2(őľ), so its gradient energy tends to zero. This proves closability of E.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.7
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P_t be the semigroup associated with the closed form (E,D(E)) in L^2(őĹ). Then, for every t>0, P_t:L^2(őĹ)‚ÜíL^2(őĹ) is Hilbert-Schmidt. Therefore there exists p_t‚ąąL^2(őĹ‚äóőĹ) such that P_t ŌÜ(x)=‚ąę_H ŌÜ(y)p_t(x,y)őĹ(dy) for őĹ-a.e. x and all ŌÜ‚ąąL^2(őĹ).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Use the minimax principle for nonnegative self-adjoint operators with compact resolvent. The Gaussian operator associated with E_0 has eigenvalues őī_n^0 given by the nondecreasing enumeration of the őõ_ő≥. Because dőĹ/dőľ is bounded above and below, Rayleigh quotients for E and E_0 are comparable: constants c,C give c őī_n^0‚Č§őī_n‚Č§C őī_n^0 for the eigenvalues őī_n of the generator associated with E. Proposition 2.2 gives ő£_n exp(-2t c őī_n^0)<‚ąě, hence ő£_n exp(-2tőī_n)<‚ąě. This is exactly the Hilbert-Schmidt criterion for P_t. A symmetric Hilbert-Schmidt operator on L^2(őĹ) has an L^2(őĹ‚äóőĹ) kernel, giving p_t.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every őĽ>0, the resolvent R_őĽ=‚ąę_0^‚ąě exp(-őĽt)P_t dt associated with E has a measurable kernel ŌĀ_őĽ(x,dy) such that R_őĽ ŌÜ(x)=‚ąę_H ŌÜ(y)ŌĀ_őĽ(x,dy) for őĹ-a.e. x and all ŌÜ‚ąąL^2(őĹ). Moreover, after defining the kernel on an exceptional set as in the proof, ŌĀ_őĽ(x,dy) is absolutely continuous with respect to őĹ(dy) for every x‚ąąH.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Dirichlet-form theory gives, outside a set N of zero capacity, a Markov transition kernel p_t(x,dy) whose action on L^2 functions has quasi-continuous versions on a closed nest. Proposition 2.7 shows that for each t>0 the semigroup also has an L^2 density p_t(x,y) with respect to őĹ for őĹ-a.e. x. Integrating these densities in time gives a őĹ‚äóőĹ-a.e. density ŌĀ_őĽ(x,y)=‚ąę_0^‚ąě exp(-őĽt)p_t(x,y)dt for the resolvent, hence absolute continuity for őĹ-a.e. x. If B is őĹ-null, then R_őĽ 1_B=0 őĹ-a.e.; the quasi-continuous version is continuous on each set in the nest and vanishes on a dense subset there, so it vanishes for all x outside N. The kernel is set identically zero on N, giving absolute continuity for all x in the stated sense.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The closed Dirichlet form (E,D(E)) on L^2(őĹ), where őĹ is the bounded-density Gibbs perturbation of Brownian bridge law described above, is quasi-regular.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The graph norms of E and E_0 are equivalent because dőĹ/dőľ is bounded above and below. Quasi-regularity is stable under such equivalence of Dirichlet-form norms. The Gaussian Dirichlet form E_0 is quasi-regular because it is associated with the continuous Ornstein-Uhlenbeck stochastic heat equation. Therefore E is quasi-regular.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (u_t)_{t‚Č•0} be the Markov process associated with (E,D(E)). Almost surely, for Lebesgue-a.e. time t, the path őł‚Ü¶u_t(őł) has a family of local times ‚Ąď^a_{t,r}, indexed by r‚ąą[0,1] and a‚ąąR, which is jointly continuous in (r,a) and satisfies ‚ąę_0^r g(u_t(s))ds=‚ąę_R g(a)‚Ąď^a_{t,r}da for bounded Borel g.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Under őľ, the coordinate path is a Brownian bridge, hence a continuous semimartingale in the spatial parameter and has jointly continuous local times. Since őĹ is equivalent to őľ, the same path property holds őĹ-a.s. Let S be the full-őĹ-measure set of paths with such local times. By Proposition 2.7, for every positive time the transition law of u_t from a fixed nonexceptional initial point is absolutely continuous with respect to őĹ, so P_x(u_t‚ąąS^c)=0 for a.e. t. Integrating over t shows that the process spends zero Lebesgue time in S^c almost surely.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let ő≤ be a standard Brownian bridge on [0,1] with law őľ, let ŌĀ(x)=exp(-F(x)) with F(x)=‚ąę_0^1 f(x_r)dr, and let f(da) denote the signed Stieltjes measure associated with the bounded-variation function f. For every h‚ąąD(A) and every ŌÜ‚ąąC_b^1(H), E[ŌĀ(ő≤) ‚ąā_h ŌÜ(ő≤)] = E[ŌĀ(ő≤) ŌÜ(ő≤)(-‚ü®h'',ő≤‚ü© + ‚ąę_{R√ó[0,1]} f(da) h_r ‚Ąď^a(dr))], where ‚Ąď^a(r) is the Brownian bridge local time accumulated over [0,r] and ‚ąā_h is the directional derivative in direction h.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Use the occupation-time formula to express F(ő≤+őĶh) in terms of the Brownian bridge local times and the Stieltjes measure f(da). Differentiating at őĶ=0 gives the local-time expression for the derivative of F along h, with the sign convention displayed in the paper. Then apply the Cameron-Martin formula for Brownian bridge shifts by őĶh: E[ŌĀ(ő≤)ŌÜ(ő≤+őĶh)] equals E[ŌĀ(ő≤-őĶh)ŌÜ(ő≤) exp(-őĶ‚ü®h'',ő≤‚ü©-őĶ^2‚ą•h‚ą•^2/2)]. Differentiating at őĶ=0 yields the integration-by-parts formula.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let U_h(x)=‚ü®x,h‚ü© for h‚ąąC_c^2((0,1)). In the Fukushima decomposition U_h(u_t)-U_h(u_0)=M^{[U_h]}_t+N^{[U_h]}_t for the Markov process associated with E, there is an exceptional set N such that for every initial point x‚ąąH‚ąĖN, P_x-a.s. for all t‚Č•0, N^{[U_h]}_t = 1/2‚ąę_0^t ‚ü®h'',u_s‚ü©ds + 1/2‚ąę_{]0,t]√ó[0,1]}‚ąę_R f(da) h'_r ‚Ąď^a_{s,r} ds dr. The local times are understood through the identity -‚ąę_0^1‚ąę_R h'_r Ōē(a)‚Ąď^a_{s,r}da dr = ‚ąę_0^1 h_r Ōē(u_s(r))dr for bounded continuous Ōē, whenever this notation is used.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Apply Fukushima decomposition to U_h‚ąąD(E). Proposition 3.4 identifies the distributional divergence of the gradient direction h, hence the Revuz measure of the zero-energy additive functional N^{[U_h]} is 1/2 times the signed measure ő£_h(dw)=(‚ü®w,h''‚ü©-‚ąę_{R√ó[0,1]} f(da)h_r d‚Ąď^a_r)őĹ(dw). The signed measure is handled by Jordan decomposition and finite-energy estimates: the local-time term is in L^2(őĹ) because f has bounded variation, h is bounded, and Brownian bridge local times have finite moments. Measures of finite energy are smooth, so each positive component has a unique positive continuous additive functional. Truncating the density and using monotone convergence shows that the PCAF is the time integral of the corresponding path functional. Finally, integration by parts in the spatial variable r converts ‚ąę h_r d‚Ąď^a_r into -‚ąę h'_r ‚Ąď^a_r dr, producing the displayed formula.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For the martingale additive functional M^{[U_h]} in the Fukushima decomposition of U_h(x)=‚ü®x,h‚ü©, there exists a Brownian sheet W(t,őł), t‚Č•0 and őł‚ąą[0,1], such that M^{[U_h]}_t=‚ąę_0^t‚ąę_0^1 h_őł W(ds,dőł) for every h‚ąąH.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Use the carr√©-du-champ formula for the Revuz measure of the quadratic variation of a martingale additive functional: ‚ąę f dőľ_{‚ü®M^{[U]}‚ü©}=2E(Uf,U)-E(U^2,f) for bounded test f in the form domain. For U_h(x)=‚ü®x,h‚ü© this gives őľ_{‚ü®M^{[U_h]}‚ü©}=‚ą•h‚ą•_H^2 őĹ, hence ‚ü®M^{[U_h]}‚ü©_t=‚ą•h‚ą•_H^2 t. Polarization gives cross-variations ‚ü®M^{[U_{h_1}]},M^{[U_{h_2}]}‚ü©_t=t‚ü®h_1,h_2‚ü©. Levy's characterization then identifies the family as an isonormal cylindrical Brownian motion over H. Taking h=1_{[0,őł]} defines W(t,őł), and the general integral representation follows by isometry.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The Dirichlet form (E,D(E)) is quasi-regular, and the Markov process associated with it is a weak solution, in the sense of Definition 1.1 of the paper, of the singular SPDE ‚ąāu/‚ąāt = 1/2 ‚ąā^2u/‚ąāőł^2 - 1/2 ‚ąę_R f(da) ‚ąā_őł ‚Ąď^a_{t,őł} + white noise, with u(t,0)=u(t,1)=0. The process is őĹ-symmetric and therefore őĹ is an invariant measure.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Quasi-regularity is Lemma 3.2, which gives an associated Markov process. Apply Fukushima decomposition to all linear functionals U_h(x)=‚ü®x,h‚ü© with h‚ąąC_c^2((0,1)). Proposition 3.5 identifies the zero-energy part as the deterministic heat drift plus the local-time drift; Proposition 3.6 identifies the martingale part as integration against a Brownian sheet. This is exactly the weak formulation against test functions h. The required space-time continuity is first obtained in stationarity from Lemma 6.1, using Sobolev moment estimates for Brownian bridge under őĹ, and then extended to nonstationary initial points outside an exceptional set using the absolute-continuity property of Proposition 2.5 and the Markov property.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemmas 4.1, 4.2, and 4.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let f_n be smooth bounded approximations of the bounded-variation f as in Section 4, define F_n(x)=‚ąę_0^1 f_n(x_r)dr and őĹ_n(dx)=Z_n^{-1}exp(-F_n(x))őľ(dx). The L^2(őĹ_n) and L^2(őĹ) norms are uniformly equivalent because all densities are bounded above and below. The forms E_n(ŌÜ,Ōą)=1/2‚ąę‚ü®‚ąáŌÜ,‚ąáŌą‚ü©dőĹ_n on K=Exp_A(H) are closable in L^2(őĹ_n). With the natural identification maps ő¶_n:L^2(őĹ)‚ÜíL^2(őĹ_n), the Hilbert spaces L^2(őĹ_n) converge to L^2(őĹ) in the sense of Kuwae-Shioya; moreover, in this bounded-density situation, strong convergence in that sense is equivalent to convergence in L^2(őĹ), and weak convergence is equivalent to ordinary weak convergence in L^2(őĹ).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Uniform norm equivalence follows immediately from boundedness of f_n and f. Closability of E_n is the same argument as Lemma 2.4, comparing to the Gaussian form E_0. For convergence of Hilbert spaces, dominated convergence gives ‚ą•ő¶_n u‚ą•_{L^2(őĹ_n)}‚Üí‚ą•u‚ą•_{L^2(őĹ)} because dőĹ_n/dőľ‚ÜídőĹ/dőľ a.s. and uniformly bounded. Strong convergence in Kuwae-Shioya's definition reduces to L^2(őĹ) convergence by norm equivalence. For weak convergence, one direction is tested against v_n=v ŌĀ ŌĀ_n^{-1}, where ŌĀ=dőĹ/dőľ and ŌĀ_n=dőĹ_n/dőľ, while the converse uses w_n=v_n ŌĀ_n ŌĀ^{-1}; dominated convergence and the strong-convergence characterization complete the equivalence.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.6 (Kuwae-Shioya, quoted)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a sequence of closed quadratic forms E_n on Hilbert spaces H_n converging to a Hilbert space H in the Kuwae-Shioya sense, Mosco convergence of E_n to a closed form E is equivalent to strong convergence of the associated resolvent operators.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper quotes this result from Kuwae and Shioya and uses it as a black box. Its role in the paper is to convert variational Mosco convergence of Dirichlet forms with changing reference measures into strong convergence of semigroups and resolvents, which is then used for convergence of stationary processes.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.7
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For the smooth approximating potentials f_n of Section 4 and the associated closed Dirichlet forms (E_n,D(E_n)) on L^2(őĹ_n), the forms E_n Mosco-converge to E on L^2(őĹ), in the varying-Hilbert-space sense of Kuwae-Shioya. In the proof the smooth approximations are used so that the measures f_n'(a)da converge weakly to the Stieltjes measure f(da).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Mosco II is immediate: for u‚ąąD(E), take u_n=u under the natural identification and use dominated convergence of the densities to get E_n(u,u)‚ÜíE(u,u); for u‚ąČD(E), both sides are infinite. For Mosco I, take u_n weakly convergent to u. If the liminf of E_n(u_n,u_n) is infinite there is nothing to prove; otherwise the uniform equivalence of E_n with E_0 gives bounded E_0 energy, and compactness of D(E_0) into L^2(őľ), plus norm equivalence, yields strong L^2 convergence along a subsequence. For test functions v in the exponential core, use the integration-by-parts formula for E and the analogous smooth formula for E_n. The terms involving Tr(D^2v) and the Gaussian drift pass to the limit by strong L^2 convergence and density convergence; the nonlinear terms pass by the occupation-time formula and weak convergence f_n'(a)da‚Üíf(da). Thus E(u,v)=lim E_n(u_n,v). The inequality E_n(u_n,u_n)^{1/2}‚Č•E_n(u_n,v)/E_n(v,v)^{1/2}, followed by convergence and taking the supremum over the core, gives E(u,u)‚Č§liminf E_n(u_n,u_n). If u‚ąČD(E), bounded liminf would force u‚ąąD(E) by lower semicontinuity, a contradiction.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.8
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P^n_{őĹ_n} be the law of the stationary solution of the smooth reaction-diffusion SPDE ‚ąāu_n/‚ąāt=1/2 ‚ąā^2u_n/‚ąāőł^2 - 1/2 f_n'(u_n)+white noise with Dirichlet boundary conditions and invariant measure őĹ_n. Let P_őĹ be the law of the stationary Markov process associated with E. Then P^n_{őĹ_n} converges weakly to P_őĹ in C([0,T]√ó[0,1]) for every T>0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Finite-dimensional distributions follow from Mosco convergence and Theorem 4.6: the semigroups P_t^n converge strongly to P_t, and repeated use of the Markov property gives convergence of expectations of products f_1(X_{t_1})...f_m(X_{t_m}) for bounded continuous cylinder functions. Tightness in C([0,T];C^őł([0,1])) is obtained uniformly in n. Since őĹ_n is uniformly equivalent to Brownian bridge law, Brownian bridge Sobolev estimates give sup_n‚ąę‚ą•x‚ą•_{W^{ő∑,p}}^pőĹ_n(dx)<‚ąě for ő∑<1/2 and suitable p. Lemma 6.1 then gives uniform temporal H√∂lder-moment estimates in a spatial H√∂lder norm, and Kolmogorov's criterion yields tightness. Combining finite-dimensional convergence and tightness gives weak convergence in C([0,T]√ó[0,1]).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For the one-dimensional skew Brownian motion X_t=X_0+B_t+ő≤L_t^0 with |ő≤|<1, define őĪ‚ąąR by (1-exp(-őĪ))/(1+exp(-őĪ))=ő≤. Then X is associated with the Dirichlet form D(u)=1/2‚ąę_R (u'(x))^2 exp(-őĪ 1_{(-‚ąě,0]}(x)) dx in L^2(exp(-őĪ 1_{(-‚ąě,0]})dx).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The weighted form is equivalent to the standard Brownian Dirichlet form, hence is closable and regular. An integration-by-parts formula for the discontinuous weight produces a boundary term at 0: ‚ąęŌÜ' exp(-őĪ1_{(-‚ąě,0]})dx=-(1-exp(-őĪ))ŌÜ(0), equivalently a local-time coefficient 2(1-exp(-őĪ))/(1+exp(-őĪ)) after normalization by the symmetric speed measure. Fukushima decomposition then shows the associated Hunt process solves the skew Brownian SDE for quasi-every initial point. The explicit transition density of skew Brownian motion gives absolute continuity, allowing extension to every initial point.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume f has the finite-jump form f(y)=f_0(y)+ő£_{j=1}^k őĪ_j 1_{y‚Č§y_j}, with f_0 smooth and bounded. Let H_n be the space of functions constant on dyadic intervals of length 2^{-n}, let őľ_n be the law of the projection P_nő≤ of a Brownian bridge, and define ŌÄ_n(dx)=Z_n^{-1}exp(-2^{-n}ő£_i f(x(i))) őľ_n(dx). The finite-dimensional form őõ_n(u,v)=1/2‚ąę‚ü®‚ąáu,‚ąáv‚ü©_{H_n} dŌÄ_n is a regular Dirichlet form in L^2(ŌÄ_n). Its associated Markov process is a weak solution of the interacting skew SDE dX_i=1/2((A_nX)_i-f_0'(X_i))dt + ő£_j ((1-exp(-őĪ_j2^{-n}))/(1+exp(-őĪ_j2^{-n}))) d‚Ąď_t^{i,y_j} + dw_t^i, and this solution is unique in law.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Regularity follows because őõ_n is equivalent to a finite-dimensional Ornstein-Uhlenbeck Dirichlet form. An integration-by-parts formula for ŌÄ_n contains the smooth drift term, the Gaussian linear drift A_n, and surface measures ŌÄ_n(dx;x(i)=y_j) coming from jumps of f. Fukushima decomposition identifies the bounded-variation additive functional with these Revuz measures. Tanaka's formula for |X_i-y_j| identifies the surface-measure PCAF with the local time ‚Ąď_t^{i,y_j} of the coordinate process. For uniqueness in law, use Girsanov to remove the bounded smooth drift 1/2(A_nX-f_0'(X)); under the changed measure the coordinates are independent skew Brownian motions with the displayed skew parameters. Harrison-Shepp pathwise uniqueness for skew Brownian motion gives uniqueness in law, and absolute continuity of skew Brownian transition kernels removes exceptional initial points.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 5.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the finite-dimensional approximation setting, the Hilbert spaces L^2(ŌÄ_n) converge to L^2(őĹ) in the Kuwae-Shioya sense. The identification map ő¶_n:L^2(őĹ)‚ÜíL^2(ŌÄ_n) is defined by conditional expectation with respect to the dyadic ŌÉ-field generated by the projected Brownian bridge values.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let ő≤ be the Brownian bridge and ő≤_n=P_nő≤. For ŌÜ‚ąąL^2(őľ), define ő¶_nŌÜ as E[ŌÜ(ő≤) | ŌÉ(ő≤_{i2^{-n}}, i=1,...,2^n)], viewed as a function of ő≤_n. This martingale converges to ŌÜ in L^2(őľ). The weights exp(-F_n(ő≤_n)) converge a.s. to exp(-F(ő≤)): f_n or the finite-dimensional sampling converges pointwise outside the finite discontinuity set of f, and Brownian bridge level sets at fixed levels have zero Lebesgue measure. Uniform boundedness of the weights gives uniform integrability, so ‚ą•ő¶_nŌÜ‚ą•_{L^2(ŌÄ_n)}‚Üí‚ą•ŌÜ‚ą•_{L^2(őĹ)}. Since all densities are uniformly comparable, the same map works for L^2(őĹ) and L^2(ŌÄ_n).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.7
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let u_n‚ąąL^2(ŌÄ_n) converge weakly to u‚ąąL^2(őĹ) in the varying-Hilbert-space sense, and assume liminf_n őõ_n(u_n,u_n)<‚ąě. Then there exists a subsequence such that u_n‚ąėP_n converges to u in L^2(őĹ).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Pass to a subsequence with uniformly bounded őõ_n(u_n,u_n). Formula (5.4) identifies őõ_n(u_n,u_n) with the Gaussian gradient energy of u_n‚ąėP_n up to uniformly bounded density factors, so E_0(u_n‚ąėP_n,u_n‚ąėP_n) is uniformly bounded. By compactness of D(E_0) into L^2(őľ), a further subsequence converges strongly in L^2(őĹ). The definition of the identification maps ő¶_n and weak convergence force the limit to be u, giving the claimed subsequential strong convergence.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5.6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For the finite-dimensional forms őõ_n associated with a potential f=f_0+ő£_j őĪ_j1_{y‚Č§y_j} as in Theorem 5.2, őõ_n Mosco-converges to the infinite-dimensional form E on L^2(őĹ).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Mosco II is verified on the core Exp_A(H): for v in the core, take v_n=v restricted to H_n. The conditional-expectation identification shows v_n‚Üív strongly, and dominated convergence plus P_n‚ąáv‚Üí‚ąáv yields őõ_n(v_n,v_n)‚ÜíE(v,v). The paper uses the Andres-von Renesse variant in which this core recovery sequence suffices. For Mosco I, take u_n weakly convergent to u with bounded liminf energy. Lemma 5.7 gives strong L^2 convergence along a subsequence. The proof introduces smooth monotone regularizations of the jump potential and auxiliary forms with measures bounded by ŌÄ_n. For v_n=v|_{H_n}, the dual inequality őõ_n(u_n,u_n)‚Č•(tildeőõ_n(u_n,v_n))^2/tildeőõ_n(v_n,v_n) is used. Integration by parts reduces convergence of tildeőõ_n(u_n,v_n) to convergence of drift terms. Smooth terms converge by strong L^2 convergence and dominated convergence. Jump terms are approximated by nŌĀ'(n(ő≤-y)); Brownian bridge modulus estimates and standard Brownian local-time continuity estimates show these approximations converge in L^2 to the local-time term in E. The discrete Gaussian drift term involving A_nP_nh is handled by showing the linear functionals g‚Ü¶‚ąęg‚ąėP_n‚ü®¬∑,A_nP_nh‚ü©dőľ are uniformly bounded and converge weakly to g‚Ü¶‚ąęg‚ü®¬∑,Ah‚ü©dőľ. Supremizing over core test functions gives the Mosco liminf inequality; if u were outside D(E), bounded liminf would contradict lower semicontinuity.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.8
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Q^n_{ŌÄ_n} be the law of the stationary finite-dimensional process associated with őõ_n and invariant measure ŌÄ_n. Then Q^n_{ŌÄ_n} converges weakly to P_őĹ, the stationary infinite-dimensional process associated with E, in C([0,T];H^{-1}(0,1)).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Define the isometry J:H^{-1}(0,1)‚ÜíL^2(0,1) by Jx(őł)=‚ü®x,1_{[0,őł]}‚ü©. The laws of JX_t^n satisfy the spatial Sobolev moment condition of Lemma 6.1 uniformly, because ŌÄ_n is a bounded perturbation of projected Brownian bridge law and J maps into H^1. Lemma 6.1 gives tightness of the transformed laws in C([0,T]√ó[0,1]), hence tightness of Q^n_{ŌÄ_n} in C([0,T];H^{-1}). Finite-dimensional distributions converge by Mosco convergence of őõ_n and the strong semigroup convergence; the Markov property reduces products at multiple times to iterated semigroups, and multiplication by bounded continuous functions preserves strong convergence in the varying-Hilbert-space sense. Tightness plus finite-dimensional convergence proves weak convergence.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 6.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let ő≥ be a probability measure on H and let (D,D(D)) be a Dirichlet form in L^2(ő≥) with C_b^1(H) as a core and D(u,v)=1/2‚ąę‚ü®‚ąáu,‚ąáv‚ü©dő≥ on the core. Let (X_t) be the stationary Markov process associated with D. Suppose there exist ő∑‚ąą(0,1), ő∂>0, and p>1 such that ő∂>1/(1+2ő∑/3), p>max{2/(1-ő∂), 1/(ő∑-(3/2)(1-ő∂)/ő∂)}, and ‚ąę_H ‚ą•x‚ą•_{W^{ő∑,p}(0,1)}^p ő≥(dx)<‚ąě. Then there exist őł‚ąą(0,1), őĺ>1, and K>0, depending only on ő∑,ő∂,p and the moment bound, such that E[‚ą•X_t-X_s‚ą•_{C^őł([0,1])}^p]‚Č§K|t-s|^őĺ for all s,t.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: First obtain temporal increments in H^{-1}(0,1). The Lyons-Zheng decomposition writes ‚ü®h,X_t-X_0‚ü© as one half of a forward martingale minus one half of a reversed martingale, both with quadratic variation t‚ą•h‚ą•^2. Burkholder-Davis-Gundy applied to the sine basis of H gives (E‚ą•X_t-X_s‚ą•_{H^{-1}}^p)^{1/p}‚Č§C|t-s|^{1/2}. Stationarity and the assumed ő≥-moment give a uniform bound on (E‚ą•X_t-X_s‚ą•_{W^{ő∑,p}}^p)^{1/p}. Interpolate between H^{-1} and W^{ő∑,p}: with őĪ=ő∂ő∑-(1-ő∂)>0 and 1/d=ő∂/p+(1-ő∂)/2, the parameter assumptions ensure őĪd>1 and őĺ=p(1-ő∂)/2>1. The Sobolev embedding W^{őĪ,d}(0,1)‚äāC^őł([0,1]) for some őł>0 then yields the displayed H√∂lder-norm moment estimate.

proof_techniques.name: Gibbs measure and symmetric Dirichlet form for a singular drift
proof_techniques.description: The paper starts from a bounded bounded-variation potential f and defines F(x)=‚ąę_0^1 f(x_r)dr and őĹ(dx)=Z^{-1}exp(-F(x))őľ(dx), where őľ is Brownian bridge law. The process is then constructed from the symmetric gradient Dirichlet form E(ŌÜ,Ōą)=1/2‚ąę‚ü®‚ąáŌÜ,‚ąáŌą‚ü©dőĹ. For smooth f, the associated SPDE is the reaction-diffusion equation with drift -1/2 f'(u); for discontinuous f, the derivative f(da) enters through spatial local times, producing the skew/local-time SPDE. Because the form is symmetric in L^2(őĹ), őĹ is automatically reversible and invariant for the constructed Markov process.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The drift őī_0(u) can be viewed formally as -1/2 f'(u) for a Heaviside-type bounded potential with a jump at 0. This suggests a concrete candidate invariant measure for the original SPDE and a way to verify invariance by identifying the ABLM solution with the corresponding Dirichlet-form process or with limits of smooth reversible approximations.

proof_techniques.name: Bounded-density transfer from Brownian bridge Ornstein-Uhlenbeck theory
proof_techniques.description: Since f is bounded, őĹ is equivalent to Brownian bridge law őľ with density bounded above and below. The paper repeatedly transfers analytic properties of the Gaussian Ornstein-Uhlenbeck Dirichlet form to the perturbed non-log-concave form using this norm equivalence. This gives closability, quasi-regularity, compactness of the form domain embedding, comparison of eigenvalues by the minimax principle, and Hilbert-Schmidt bounds for the perturbed semigroup.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the őī_0 drift is represented by a bounded jump potential, the same bounded-density comparison should be available. It can supply compactness and transition-density information even though the potential is non-convex and standard monotonicity or log-concavity arguments are unavailable.

proof_techniques.name: Absolute continuity of the resolvent without strong Feller
proof_techniques.description: The paper notes that strong Feller is not available, but proves a weaker absolute-continuity property. First, Hilbert-Schmidt bounds give L^2 transition densities p_t(x,y) for őĹ-a.e. x. Second, quasi-regular Dirichlet-form theory gives quasi-continuous versions of resolvent potentials outside an exceptional set. Combining these, if B is őĹ-null then R_őĽ1_B vanishes őĹ-a.e. and hence, by quasi-continuity on a nest, vanishes for all nonexceptional starting points; the kernel is defined arbitrarily on the exceptional set.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For uniqueness of invariant measures, a common route is to combine absolute continuity with some form of irreducibility or full support. This paper supplies the absolute-continuity half in a singular, non-strong-Feller setting; a solver could look for an additional positivity/irreducibility argument for the őī_0 equation.

proof_techniques.name: Integration by parts using Brownian bridge local times
proof_techniques.description: The core analytic identity is an integration-by-parts formula for the weighted Brownian bridge measure exp(-F)őľ. The derivative of F along a Cameron-Martin direction h is expressed through the occupation-time formula and the Brownian bridge local times ‚Ąď^a(r), while the Gaussian shift contributes the term -‚ü®h'',ő≤‚ü©. This converts the formal distributional derivative f'(u) into a rigorous local-time term involving the Stieltjes measure f(da).
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a drift őī_0(u), the relevant potential has a jump at 0, so the corresponding integration-by-parts formula should involve Brownian bridge local time at level 0. This is the main calculation needed to identify the generator and to verify that a proposed Gibbs measure is infinitesimally invariant.

proof_techniques.name: Fukushima decomposition and Revuz-measure identification of SPDE terms
proof_techniques.description: After constructing the symmetric Markov process from E, the paper applies Fukushima decomposition to linear observables U_h(x)=‚ü®x,h‚ü©. The zero-energy additive functional is identified by computing its Revuz measure through the integration-by-parts formula, and then representing the corresponding smooth measures as time integrals of path functionals. The martingale additive functional is identified as a cylindrical Brownian motion by computing its quadratic variation through the carr√©-du-champ formula.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is a robust way to prove that a Dirichlet-form process solves a singular SPDE without pointwise drift. It could be used to compare the ABLM weak mild solution with a reversible Dirichlet-form solution for the őī_0 potential.

proof_techniques.name: Mosco convergence of regularized singular equations
proof_techniques.description: The paper approximates the bounded-variation potential f by smooth bounded f_n and studies the reversible smooth SPDEs with invariant measures őĹ_n‚ąĚexp(-‚ąęf_n)dőľ. It proves Mosco convergence E_n‚ÜíE using integration by parts, compactness of the Gaussian form domain, and convergence of the distributional derivatives f_n'(a)da to f(da). Kuwae-Shioya's theorem then turns Mosco convergence into strong resolvent and semigroup convergence, which gives convergence of stationary laws.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem already assumes convergence of smooth approximations with drift G_{1/n}. One possible strategy is to identify invariant measures for the approximations, show they converge to the Gibbs measure for the jump potential, and pass invariance or ergodic properties to the limiting semigroup.

proof_techniques.name: Stationary tightness via Lyons-Zheng and Sobolev interpolation
proof_techniques.description: The a priori estimate in Lemma 6.1 obtains time regularity of stationary processes associated with gradient Dirichlet forms without using explicit SPDE estimates. Lyons-Zheng decomposition gives H^{-1} increments of order |t-s|^{1/2}; stationarity gives uniform spatial W^{ő∑,p} bounds from the invariant measure; interpolation and Sobolev embedding yield temporal H√∂lder estimates in a spatial H√∂lder norm. This is used for tightness of both smooth and finite-dimensional stationary approximations.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If one needs compactness of invariant or stationary laws for őī_0 approximations on C_0([0,1]), this estimate provides a model route: prove uniform Brownian-bridge-type Sobolev moments under candidate invariant measures, then use Dirichlet-form martingale decomposition for time regularity.

proof_techniques.name: Finite-dimensional skew Brownian approximations
proof_techniques.description: For potentials with finitely many jumps, the finite-dimensional projections are systems of interacting skew Brownian motions. The jump of the potential produces coordinate local times with explicit skew parameters. The paper proves regularity of the finite-dimensional Dirichlet forms, identifies the SDEs by integration by parts and Tanaka formula, and proves uniqueness in law by a Girsanov transform to independent skew Brownian motions plus Harrison-Shepp uniqueness.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Finite-dimensional approximations may be useful for proving irreducibility, support properties, or uniqueness of invariant measures at the approximate level before passing to the limit. The explicit skew Brownian structure is especially relevant when the őī_0 drift is encoded by a jump potential.

proof_techniques.name: Core-recovery Mosco convergence for changing finite-dimensional spaces
proof_techniques.description: In the finite-dimensional approximation, the paper uses an Andres-von Renesse variant of Mosco convergence: instead of constructing recovery sequences for every domain element directly, it suffices to construct them on a core Exp_A(H). Restrictions of exponential cylinder functions to H_n are the recovery sequence. The liminf inequality is obtained through compactness and duality against this core.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a proof of invariant-measure uniqueness or invariance uses Galerkin approximations, this gives a practical method for proving semigroup convergence without needing dense explicit approximations of every infinite-dimensional Sobolev function.

other_useful_info: The introduction explicitly says that for general bounded-variation f, uniqueness of weak solutions to the singular SPDE seems out of reach; the constructed process is called canonical because it is associated with the explicit Dirichlet form and obtained as a limit of natural regularizations and discretizations. The authors also emphasize that the invariant Gibbs measure is generally non-log-concave, and that strong Feller is not proved; Proposition 2.5 is presented as a weaker substitute. For smooth f, the paper states that equation (1.6) is equivalent by occupation times to the reaction-diffusion SPDE ‚ąāu/‚ąāt=1/2‚ąā^2u/‚ąāőł^2 -1/2 f'(u)+white noise, which is the key bridge between distributional value-drifts and the local-time formulation. Since f is bounded, őĹ is equivalent to Brownian bridge law, so all Brownian-bridge almost sure path properties used in the paper, including continuity, local times, and Sobolev moment bounds, transfer to őĹ. External tools cited and used include Fukushima-Oshima-Takeda and Ma-Rockner for Dirichlet forms, Da Prato-Zabczyk for the Gaussian stochastic heat equation, Kuwae-Shioya and Kolesnikov for Mosco convergence with varying Hilbert spaces, Andres-von Renesse for a core version of Mosco convergence, and Harrison-Shepp for skew Brownian uniqueness.
why_relevant: This paper studies a closely related stochastic heat equation with a singular skew/local-time drift and gives an explicit Gibbs-type invariant measure plus Dirichlet-form and approximation constructions. It is one of the most directly relevant sources for identifying possible invariant measures and proving uniqueness for the őī0-drift equation.
